questions.question: Define a structure in C and a union in C. Explain the fundamental difference between them in terms of memory allocation.
keyPoints: A structure is a user-defined data type that groups variables of different data types under a single name. Each member has its own memory location.
A union is also a user-defined data type that groups variables, but all members share the same memory location.
The key difference: In a structure, the total memory allocated is at least the sum of the sizes of all its members. In a union, the memory allocated is equal to the size of its largest member.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the use cases for structures and unions. Provide a concrete example scenario where using a union is more memory-efficient than using a structure.
keyPoints: Structures are used to represent a record that contains multiple related attributes, all of which need to be stored simultaneously (e.g., a 'Student' record with name, ID, and GPA).
Unions are used when only one member needs to be active at any given time, saving significant memory. They are often used in interpreter design, protocol packets, or hardware register access.

Example Scenario: Creating a variable to store data that can be either an integer, a float, or a character string. A union would allocate memory only for the largest type (e.g., the string), while a structure would allocate memory for all three, wasting space.

questions.question: A programmer needs to create a data type to represent different geometric shapes (e.g., circle, rectangle, triangle) with their respective attributes. Design a solution using a union embedded within a structure. Explain the purpose of the 'tag' field in the outer structure and detail how this implementation manages memory. Discuss one potential pitfall of this approach.
keyPoints: Solution: Create a struct containing a 'tag' field (an enum) to identify the active shape and a union containing structs for each shape type (e.g., circle with radius, rectangle with length/width, triangle with base/height).
Purpose of the tag: The tag field is crucial for keeping track of which member of the union is currently being used, allowing for safe access and interpretation of the data stored in the union.

Memory Management: The union ensures only enough memory is allocated for the largest shape structure (e.g., rectangle and triangle might be larger than circle), optimizing memory usage for the shape data.

Potential Pitfall: The programmer must diligently check the tag field before accessing a union member. Accessing the wrong member (e.g., reading 'radius' when the tag is 'rectangle') leads to erroneous data interpretation and logical errors, as the memory is reinterpreted incorrectly.
